attachment.content:
- update-config: Use this skill to configure the Claude Code harness via settings.json. Automated behaviors ("from now on when X", "each time X", "whenever X", "before/after X") require hooks configured in settings.json - the harness executes these, not Claude, so memory/preferences cannot fulfill them. Also use for: permissions ("allow X", "add permission", "move permission to"), env vars ("set X=Y"), hook troubleshooting, or any changes to settings.json/settings.local.json files. Examples: "allow npm commands", "add bq permission to global settings", "move permission to user settings", "set DEBUG=true", "when claude stops show X". For simple settings like theme/model, suggest the /config command.
- keybindings-help: Use when the user wants to customize keyboard shortcuts, rebind keys, add chord bindings, or modify ~/.claude/keybindings.json. Examples: "rebind ctrl+s", "add a chord shortcut", "change the submit key", "customize keybindings".
- verify: Verify that a code change actually does what it's supposed to by running the app and observing behavior. Use when asked to verify a PR, confirm a fix works, test a change manually, check that a feature works, or validate local changes before pushing.
- code-review: Review the current diff for correctness bugs and reuse/simplification/efficiency cleanups at the given effort level (low/medium: fewer, high-confidence findings; high→max: broader coverage, may include uncertain findings). Pass --comment to post findings as inline PR comments, or --fix to apply the findings to the working tree after the review.
- simplify: Review the changed code for reuse, simplification, efficiency, and altitude cleanups, then apply the fixes. Quality only — it does not hunt for bugs; use /code-review for that.
- fewer-permission-prompts: Scan your transcripts for common read-only Bash and MCP tool calls, then add a prioritized allowlist to project .claude/settings.json to reduce permission prompts.
- loop: Run a prompt or slash command on a recurring interval (e.g. /loop 5m /foo). Omit the interval to let the model self-pace. - When the user wants to set up a recurring task, poll for status, or run something repeatedly on an interval (e.g. "check the deploy every 5 minutes", "keep running /babysit-prs"). Do NOT invoke for one-off tasks.
- schedule: Create, update, list, or run scheduled cloud agents (routines) that execute on a cron schedule. - When the user wants to schedule a recurring cloud agent, set up automated tasks, create a cron job for Claude Code, or manage their scheduled agents/routines. Also use when the user wants a one-time scheduled run ("run this once at 3pm", "remind me to check X tomorrow").
- claude-api: Reference for the Claude API / Anthropic SDK — model ids, pricing, params, streaming, tool use, MCP, agents, caching, token counting, model migration.
TRIGGER — read BEFORE opening the target file; don't skip because it "looks like a one-liner" — whenever: the prompt names Claude/Anthropic in any form (Claude, Anthropic, Fable, Opus, Sonnet, Haiku, `anthropic`, `@anthropic-ai`, `claude-*`, `us.anthropic.*`, `[1m]`); the user asks about an LLM (pricing/model choice/limits/caching) — never answer from memory; OR the task is LLM-shaped with provider unstated (agent/MCP/tool-definition/multi-agent/RAG/LLM-judge/computer-use; generate/summarize/extract/classify/rewrite/converse over NL; debugging refusals/cutoffs/streaming/tool-calls/tokens).
SKIP only when another provider is being worked on (overrides all triggers): OpenAI/GPT/Gemini/Llama/Mistral/Cohere/Ollama named in the query; OR `grep -rE 'openai|langchain_openai|google.generativeai|genai|mistralai|cohere|ollama'` over the project hits (run this grep FIRST if no provider named — don't Read the file).
- run: Launch and drive this project's app to see a change working. Use when asked to run, start, or screenshot the app, or to confirm a change works in the real app (not just tests). First looks for a project skill that already covers launching the app; otherwise falls back to built-in patterns per project type (CLI, server, TUI, Electron, browser-driven, library).
- init: Initialize a new CLAUDE.md file with codebase documentation
- review: Review a pull request
- security-review: Complete a security review of the pending changes on the current branch

content.text:
Confirmed. Here's the diagnosis.

## These aren't Pawtograder bugs — they're a browser extension

Both events are the **same root cause**, which is why they look coupled. Look at the stack frames:

```
app:///assets/onMessage-fb148085.js              ← e()
app:///assets/respondToWindowMessage-a913f053.js ← handler()
app:///assets/scrapeScript.ts-e0616051.js        ← handler()
app:///assets/index.esm-ab56d324.js              ← isReaderPage, getRawReaderData,
                                                    getRawPageByType, ...
```

None of that is your code. Pawtograder's bundles live under `app:///_next/static/...`. These `app:///assets/*.js` files — with names like `scrapeScript.ts`, `onMessage`, `respondToWindowMessage`, and functions `isReaderPage` / `getRawReaderData` / `getRawPageByType` — are a **content script injected by a browser extension** (a "reader mode" / page-scraper type extension). It runs on every page the user visits, including yours.

**Why it throws:** the extension builds this selector and feeds it to `querySelector`:

```css
body:has(div:has(> h1) + nav[aria-label="Breadcrumb"] ol li a[href="/chapters/"]):has(...)
```

That `div:has(...)` nested **inside** an outer `:has()` is illegal per the CSS spec — `:has()` cannot be nested inside another `:has()`. The browser rejects the whole string with `SyntaxError ... is not a valid selector` (DOMException code 12). It fires regardless of which route the user is on (you see it on `/assignments` and `/assignments/1020`) because the extension scrapes every navigation. The `a[href="/chapters/"]` selector confirms it's not targeting Pawtograder — you have no `/chapters/` route.

**How it reaches Sentry:** the extension's unhandled promise rejection bubbles to the page's global `onunhandledrejection`, and Sentry's `GlobalHandlers` catches it (`mechanism.type: auto.browser.global_handlers.onunhandledrejection`).

So: harmless third-party noise, not a regression in your release. The right move is to filter it — same as you already do for hydration/MetaMask/Grammarly noise in `beforeSend`.

## Recommended fix

Your `instrumentation-client.ts` already filters browser-extension hydration errors. I'll add a filter for extension-originated errors that keys off the `app:///assets/` frame origin (your real code is always `app:///_next/static/...`), which catches this whole class rather than just this one selector string.

content.content: The file /scratch/platform/instrumentation-client.ts has been updated successfully. (file state is current in your context — no need to Read it back)

lines: +    // Filter errors thrown by injected browser extensions (reader-mode/page-scraper
+    // content scripts, etc.). Our own code is always served from `app:///_next/static/...`,
+    // whereas extension content scripts surface as `app:///assets/*.js`. These bubble up to
+    // the page's global onunhandledrejection handler and get attributed to us even though
+    // they originate from the user's extensions (e.g. invalid `querySelector` selectors).

input.command: ls public/ 2>/dev/null; echo "--- any first-party .js under public/assets? ---"; find public -path '*assets*' -name '*.js' 2>/dev/null | head; echo "(empty = safe)"

content.text:
Safe — you ship no JS under `app:///assets/`, so the filter can't suppress your own errors.

## Summary

**Root cause:** Both Sentry events are the *same* error from a **user's browser extension** (a reader-mode / page-scraper content script: `scrapeScript.ts`, `onMessage`, `isReaderPage`, `getRawReaderData` from `app:///assets/*.js`). It runs `querySelector` with a CSS selector that nests `:has()` inside `:has()` — illegal per spec, so the browser throws `SyntaxError: ... is not a valid selector` (DOMException 12). It targets `a[href="/chapters/"]`, a route you don't even have. The rejection bubbles to the page's global handler and Sentry's `GlobalHandlers` attributes it to your release.

**It is not a Pawtograder regression** in release `6c930585...`, and there's nothing to fix in your app code.

**What I changed:** Added a `beforeSend` filter in `instrumentation-client.ts` that drops any event whose stack frames originate from `app:///assets/` (extension content scripts) — your first-party code is always `app:///_next/static/...`, and I verified you ship nothing under `/assets/`, so it won't hide real errors. This catches the whole class of extension noise, not just this one selector.

A couple of things worth noting:
- The frames are marked `in_app: true` (so Sentry didn't auto-classify them as third-party) because your config uses `integrations: []` for Bugsink — there's no `RewriteFrames`/in-app exclusion running. The `beforeSend` approach is the correct lever here.
- This is the same pattern as your existing hydration/MetaMask/Grammarly filters, so it fits the established convention.

If you'd rather *keep* visibility into extension errors and just triage them in Bugsink, say so and I'll revert — but given the existing filters, suppressing it is the consistent choice.

content.text: Confirmed — `order()` reads the existing `order` param and **appends** to it, mutating the shared `this.url`. Now confirm the copy constructor shares the URL object (so the since-watermark path mutates `this._query` too):

input.command: cd /scratch/platform; grep -rn -A20 "constructor(builder" node_modules/@supabase/postgrest-js/dist/cjs/PostgrestBuilder.js 2>/dev/null | head -30; echo "=== gt impl ==="; grep -n -A4 "gt(column" node_modules/@supabase/postgrest-js/dist/cjs/PostgrestFilterBuilder.js 2>/dev/null | head

content.text:
Root cause **confirmed and proven**. The copy constructor (`this.url = builder.url`) shares the URL object — it does **not** clone. And `order()`/`gt()` mutate `this.url.searchParams`. So:

- **Line 1228** (`_fetchInitialData`): `this._query.order("id", …)` mutates `this._query` directly. The comment "PostgREST query builders are immutable" is **false**. Every full refetch appends another `id.asc` → `order=id.asc,id.asc,id.asc,…`
- **Line 1128** (`_refetchSinceMaxUpdatedAt`): `new PostgrestFilterBuilder(this._query)` shares the same URL, so `.gt("updated_at",…).order("updated_at",…).order("id",…)` appends to `this._query` too → `order=id.asc,updated_at.asc.nullslast,…` plus a new `updated_at=gt.…` filter each time.

The realtime reconnect storm in the breadcrumbs (repeated `channel_error → reconnect` on `class:500`) drives a refetch each cycle. The query string grows unbounded until PostgREST rejects it with **400 Bad Request**, caught at lines 1207/1365 and reported as *"Object captured as exception with keys: message"*. All three tables in the breadcrumbs are separate controllers on the same page degrading identically — that's why they look coupled.

Let me confirm `this._query` isn't relied upon to be mutated elsewhere, then check the field decl/import:

input.command: cd /scratch/platform; grep -n "this\._query\|_query:\|_query =\|import.*PostgrestFilterBuilder\|PostgrestFilterBuilder<" lib/TableController.ts | head -40

content.text: `this._query` is consumed only at 1128 and 1228 (set once in the constructor). Let me read the field's exact generic type so the helper is typed correctly:

content.text: `url` is `protected`, so the clone needs a small cast. Now I'll add a `_freshQuery()` helper and use it at both mutation sites. First, the helper (inserted just above `_refetchSinceMaxUpdatedAt`):

content.content: The file /scratch/platform/lib/TableController.ts has been updated successfully. (file state is current in your context — no need to Read it back)

lines: +   * Return an independent copy of the base query whose URL can be mutated
+   * etc. mutate `this.url.searchParams` (appending in the case of `order`/
+   * `gt`) and return `this`. The copy constructor (`new PostgrestFilterBuilder(q)`)
+   * copies the `url` *reference* rather than cloning it, so a "derived" query
+   * still shares — and mutates — the original's URL. Deriving from `this._query`
+   * without cloning therefore leaks clauses back into `this._query`, and across
+   * repeated refetches (e.g. a realtime reconnect storm) this accumulates
+   * request grows large enough that PostgREST rejects it with 400 Bad Request.
+   * Cloning the URL here keeps `this._query` pristine for every refetch.
+    const fresh = new PostgrestFilterBuilder(this._query) as this["_query"] & { url: URL };
+    fresh.url = new URL((this._query as unknown as { url: URL }).url.toString());
* Incremental refetch using updated_at watermark if available.
* Falls back to no-op if watermark is not set or table lacks updated_at.

content.text:
Done. Here's the diagnosis and fix.

## This one is a real bug (not noise) — and yes, the three errors are coupled

All three `Object captured as exception with keys: message` events share one root cause. The tell is in the breadcrumbs: the failing requests (`review_assignments`, `repositories`, `submission_regrade_requests` — three separate `TableController`s on `/course/500`) have query strings with `order=id.asc,id.asc,id.asc,…` repeated **hundreds of times**, plus `updated_at=gt.…` repeated many times. PostgREST rejects the over-long query with **400 Bad Request**, which `TableController` catches and reports as the serialized `{message: "Bad Request"}`.

**Why the params accumulate:** PostgREST query builders are **not immutable** (I verified against the installed `@supabase/postgrest-js`):
- `order()` reads the existing `order` param and **appends** to it, mutating `this.url.searchParams`.
- `gt()` appends to `this.url.searchParams`.
- The copy constructor `new PostgrestFilterBuilder(q)` copies the `url` **reference**, not a clone (`this.url = builder.url`).

`TableController` derived its per-refetch query directly from the shared `this._query`:
- `lib/TableController.ts:1228` — `this._query.order("id", …)` (with a comment literally claiming *"PostgREST query builders are immutable"* — false), and
- `lib/TableController.ts:1128` — `new PostgrestFilterBuilder(this._query).gt("updated_at",…).order(…)`.

So **every refetch leaked another `order`/`gt` clause into the base query.** The breadcrumbs show a realtime **reconnect storm** on `class:500` (repeated `channel_error → reconnect`), and each reconnect drives a refetch → unbounded query growth → eventual 400.

## The fix

Added a `_freshQuery()` helper that clones the URL so the base query stays pristine, and routed both refetch paths through it:

- `lib/TableController.ts` — new `_freshQuery()` (clones `url` via `new URL(url.toString())`), used at the two mutation sites; corrected the misleading "immutable" comment.

## Tests (red/green verified)

`tests/unit/table-controller-query-mutation.test.ts` — 3 specs asserting the base query never accumulates `order`/`updated_at` params across 25 refetches on both the full-refetch and since-watermark paths, plus that `_freshQuery()` clones are independent. I confirmed they **fail** with the bug reintroduced and **pass** with the fix. Typecheck clean.

## Note on the earlier (separate) error

The first error you sent — the `body:has(...)` `querySelector` `SyntaxError` — is unrelated: that's third-party **browser-extension** noise (I added the `app:///assets/` filter to `beforeSend` for it). This `Bad Request` cluster is a genuine Pawtograder bug and is now fixed at the source rather than merely suppressed. (Your `beforeSend` already has a "Bad Request" filter, but suppressing it would have hidden a real runaway-query problem — fixing the accumulation is the correct move.)

I did not add a memory: this is now documented in the code comments and captured in the fix itself, so it's not a non-obvious fact that would otherwise be lost.

input.command:
cd /scratch/platform; git commit -F - <<'EOF'
Fix PostgREST query-param accumulation (400s) and filter extension noise

Two coupled Sentry production errors:

1. "Object captured as exception with keys: message" / 400 Bad Request.
   PostgREST builders are NOT immutable: .order()/.gt() append to
   this.url.searchParams, and the copy constructor copies the url
   *reference* (not a clone). TableController derived its per-refetch
   query straight from this._query, so every refetch leaked another
   order=id.asc (and updated_at=gt.…) clause into the shared base query.
   During a realtime reconnect storm the query string grew without bound
   (order=id.asc,id.asc,…) until PostgREST rejected it with 400.
   Fix: route both refetch paths through a new _freshQuery() that clones
   the URL so the base query stays pristine.

2. "Failed to execute 'querySelector' … is not a valid selector"
   (DOMException 12). Thrown by an injected browser-extension content
   script (app:///assets/*.js), not our code, and surfaced via the page's
   global onunhandledrejection. Drop events whose frames originate from
   app:///assets/ in beforeSend (our code is always _next/static).

Adds tests/unit/table-controller-query-mutation.test.ts asserting the base
query never accumulates order/updated_at params across repeated full and
since-watermark refetches (verified red/green).

Co-Authored-By: Claude Opus 4.8 (1M context) <noreply@anthropic.com>
EOF
